Summary: This study evaluates the potential of radiomics features extracted from normal-appearing white matter (NAWM) to predict the progression of multiple sclerosis (MS) on MRI. The authors trained a machine learning model (XGBoost) to differentiate between "prelesion" areas and control samples within the NAWM. The model achieved high accuracy on the test set and moderate accuracy on an external validation set, suggesting that radiomics features could serve as imaging biomarkers for MS progression.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of prelesion and control regions in the NAWM.

Condition2.reason: The study mentions using DeepMedic for baseline automated segmentation of MS lesions.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomics features using PyRadiomics, which are hand-crafted features.

Condition4.reason: The study uses radiomic features in a tabulated format for machine learning.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning approach. It relies on feature extraction followed by machine learning models.

Item1.reason: The study mentions using the Checklist for Artificial Intelligence in Medical Imaging and conforms to the Assessment of Radiomics Research (ARISE) guideline.

Item2.reason: The study defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, including having at least two MRI brain studies for MS and the presence of a new lesion on follow-up imaging.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is based on the MAGNIMS consensus guidelines for defining radiologic progression (new or enlarging lesions), which is a well-established standard.

Item4.reason: The study uses an external validation dataset (MSSEG2) in addition to their internal data.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is a standard 3D T2-FLAIR sequence, which is commonly used in MS imaging.

Item6.reason: The study reports the image acquisition parameters for the 3D T2-FLAIR sequence, including voxel size, TR, TE, TI, and acquisition time.

Item7.reason: The study uses prior MR imaging to predict future lesion occurrence, which is appropriate for validating the presence or absence of target conditions at the moment of diagnostic imaging exams.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the use of DeepMedic for baseline segmentation followed by manual correction by radiologists.

Item9.reason: The study does not provide a formal evaluation of the DeepMedic automated segmentation accuracy compared to domain expert annotations. The baseline segmentations were manually corrected.

Item10.reason: The final segmentation in the test set is produced by radiologists, reflecting clinical practice.

Item11.reason: The study mentions coregistering images using FSL and excludes shape-related radiomic features because the shapes of the control segmentations were dependent on the original lesion segmentations.

Item12.reason: The study uses PyRadiomics for feature extraction.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (radiomics features) and mentions excluding shape-related features. The github link provides access to the code.

Item14.reason: The study does not explicitly mention removing non-robust features through test-retest, reproducibility, or stability analysis.

Item15.reason: The study does not explicitly mention removing redundant features using feature selection algorithms or other methods.

Item16.reason: The study does not provide a clear justification for the appropriateness of the dimensionality compared to the data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline.

Item18.reason: The study splits the data into training, test, and external validation sets. The split is done before processing.

Item19.reason: The study mentions that failure analysis on external validation showed that in many instances, a potential cause for misclassified cases was misregistration of images rather than the machine learning model.

Item20.reason: The study reports accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for the classification task.

Item21.reason: The study does not include uncertainty measures such as confidence intervals or standard deviations.

Item22.reason: The study does not assess the calibration of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study uses only one imaging set (3D T2-FLAIR sequence).

Item24.reason: The study compares the performance of the radiomics-based model with a baseline model using only the mean intensity value of the segmented regions.

Item25.reason: The study compares the performance of the radiomics-based model with a baseline model using only the mean pixel intensity.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set that is sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The model is tested with independent data from another institution (MSSEG2).

Item28.reason: The study does not explicitly state that the imaging, segmentation, clinical, or radiomics analysis data is shared with the public, but the code is available.

Item29.reason: The study provides a link to the code used for the experiments.

Item30.reason: The study does not explicitly state that the final model is shared in the form of a raw model file or as a ready-to-use system.
